scope_note: QA Quantize-to-ONE cert [321]. Primary source: Iverson, B. (1995) Quantum Arithmetic Book 3 (QA-3), ITAM Portland, ISBN 1-883401-08-9, Chapter 3: 'Quantizing to ONE'. The 'Quantize to ONE' extension applies the Quantize algorithm when KO = THE ONE (the Myriad reference unit), so JO/KO = b/a is the measurement ratio relative to the Myriad scale. Three structural results certified: (C1) Cosmos ratio b/a in (0,1): for all 72 Cosmos pairs, b < a = b+2e (since e>=1), so the ratio is strictly less than 1 — Iverson: 'perigee divided by apogee will always be a decimal less than 1'. (C2) Satellite canonical ratio 1/3: all 8 Satellite pairs (b=e) give b/a = b/(3b) = 1/3 exactly; and 1/3 is not achieved by any Cosmos pair (b/a=1/3 implies b=e, which is the Satellite condition). (C3) Within-d-class ratio monotonicity: for fixed d=b+e, the ratio b/(2d-b) is strictly increasing in b — verified for all d-classes in {2,...,17} (mod-9 range); no two Cosmos pairs in the same d-class share a ratio. (C4) Seven-prime factorization bound: for all 72 Cosmos pairs, the number of distinct prime factors omega(b*e*d*a) <= 5 — confirming Ben's claim 'there should be no more than seven successful divisions' (p.40) with room to spare; empirical max is 5, achieved at e.g. (5,8) with product 5*8*13*21=10920=2^3*3*5*7*13. (C5) Theorem NT boundary: the ratio b/a = b/(b+2e) is the observer-layer measurement (what a sensor reports as the perigee/apogee ratio); the integer pair (b,e) is the QA causal layer; Quantize-to-ONE is the canonical inversion of the ratio observer projection. Builds on cert [320] (Quantize algorithm, DO=d^2, lossless reconstruction). DOI: primary text, no external DOI.
